source.definition: to put something down in a flat or horizontal position

explanation.sourceText: The English word "lay" comes from Old English "lecgan", meaning "to put down, to place horizontally", describing the action of placing something flat.
explanation.targetText: Cuvântul englezesc „lay“ provine din engleza veche „lecgan“, care înseamnă „a pune jos, a așeza orizontal“, descriind acțiunea de a plasa ceva plat.

source.additional: An irregular verb, commonly used to describe placing objects in a specific position.

target.definition: a plasa ceva într-o poziție orizontală

explanation.sourceText: The Romanian verb „a pune“ comes from Latin „ponere“, meaning „to put, to place“, describing the act of placing objects.
explanation.targetText: Verbul românesc „a pune“ provine din latină, de la „ponere“, care înseamnă „a pune, a așeza“.

target.additional: Regular verb of the 1st conjugation. It is often used in everyday situations to describe arranging or positioning.

explanation.sourceText: To lie down on the floor.

examples.sourceText: He lays the child in bed.

explanation.sourceText: Used to describe placing an object.
explanation.targetText: Folosit pentru a descrie plasarea unui obiect.

examples.sourceText: I lay the cup on the table.
I lay the book on the table.
Do you lay the glass here?
We lay the cards on the table.
Do you lay the things neatly?
They lay the mattresses on the bed.
I have laid the photo on the website.
He laid the stones every day.
examples.targetText: El punea pietrele în fiecare zi.

examples.sourceText: I will tidy up the toys.
I would lay the book if it were not heavy.
examples.targetText: Aș pune cartea dacă nu ar fi grea.

learningTips.text: Remember the Romanian verb „a pune“ by linking it to the English verb "put"—both mean to place something somewhere.
Think of the phrase "I put the puppy on the couch" (Eu pun cățelul pe canapea) to keep the word vivid in your mind.
Practice sentences starting with "Eu pun" and different objects to get comfortable with the verb's use.
The sound of "pune" is similar to "pony"—imagine putting a pony somewhere to remember the verb.
